name: West Frisian

stress.notes: Germanic word stress falls on the first syllable of the root; prefixes and a small set of loans are the systematic exceptions (Tiersma 1999, ch. 1). The declared sequences are the ones that form a single nucleus: the orthographic digraphs/trigraphs let the bundled syllabifier count one nucleus per diphthong, and their IPA realisations (⟨ei⟩ /ɛi/, ⟨au⟩/⟨ou⟩ /ɔu/, ⟨ui⟩ /øy/, the centring /iə ɪə oə/) are listed too so the same run stays one nucleus when the syllabifier is re-run over the transcription rather than the spelling.

ancestors.notes: Descent from Old Frisian (c. 1150–1550) via Middle Frisian (1550–1820). West Frisian is the West Frisian branch of the Anglo-Frisian sub-group.
Dutch adstrate: centuries of Dutch administrative and literary dominance in Friesland (since 1498). Heavy lexical borrowing and phonological convergence, especially in urban varieties.
Latin adstrate: ecclesiastical and learned vocabulary via Christianisation and medieval scholarship.
French adstrate: indirect influence through Dutch and Burgundian-period borrowings.

notes: West Frisian (Frysk) — the most widely spoken Frisian language, spoken in the province of Fryslân (Friesland), Netherlands, where it is an official language alongside Dutch. KEY FEATURES: (1) RICH VOWEL SYSTEM: monophthongs contrast in length and rounding, with long vowels (>250 ms) considerably longer than short (<150 ms) (Tiersma 1999, p. 9). Short ⟨a⟩ is front /a/, long ⟨aa⟩ /aː/. (2) CENTRING (BREAKING) DIPHTHONGS: ⟨ie⟩ /iə/, ⟨ea⟩ /ɪə/, ⟨oa⟩ /oə/ from historical long vowels — a hallmark of Frisian. Breaking (brekking) alternates a stressed falling diphthong with a rising one in derivation (stien /stiən/ ~ stiennen /stjɪn.../, beam /bɪəm/ ~ beamke /bjɛm.../; Tiersma 1999); this is a morphophonemic alternation not expressible in the grapheme→IPA map and is not encoded. (3) FINAL OBSTRUENT DEVOICING: word-final /b d v z ɣ/ merge to /p t f s χ/ (Tiersma 1999, p. 21). (4) ⟨g⟩ is a single phoneme with a stop [ɡ] word- and stressed-syllable-initially and a voiced velar fricative [ɣ] elsewhere, devoiced to [χ] word-finally (Hoekstra 2001). (5) DORSAL FRICATIVE: ⟨ch⟩ and the devoiced ⟨g⟩ are the voiceless post-velar/uvular fricative /χ/. (6) PALATALISATION: ⟨sj⟩ /ɕ/, ⟨tsj⟩ and ⟨tj⟩ /tɕ/ (alveolo-palatal). (7) CIRCUMFLEX VOWELS: ⟨â⟩ /ɔː/, ⟨ê⟩ /ɛː/, ⟨ô⟩ /oː/, ⟨û⟩ /uː/, ⟨ú⟩ /yː/ — a distinctive orthographic convention. (8) ⟨y⟩ = /i/ (a vowel, not a consonant); short ⟨i⟩ = /ɪ/; ⟨u⟩ = /ø/. (9) ANGLO-FRISIAN: shares innovations with English but diverged after the Old Frisian period. Sources: Tiersma (1999) *Frisian Reference Grammar*, Hoekstra (2001), Popkema (2006) *Grammatica Fries*.
